title: India's youth-led protest movement enters government talks after activist's hunger strike
dek: The Cockroach Janta Party held talks with India's government on July 24 after activist Sonam Wangchuk ended a 26-day hunger strike over exam fraud and job scarcity. The government agreed to two demands but has not committed to the protesters' primary demand: education minister Dharmendra Pradhan's resignation.
author: The GoCheckMyNews Desk
key_fact: A youth-led protest movement forced India's Modi government to the negotiating table after sustained pressure and a dramatic hunger strike by activist Sonam Wangchuk.
bottom_line: The negotiations resume Saturday with the government's response on the minister's resignation. The outcome will test whether sustained youth pressure can force personnel changes at the cabinet level, or whether partial concessions are sufficient to defuse the movement. The September date for parliamentary debate on exam reforms will signal how seriously the government intends to address the underlying grievances.
body: The Cockroach Janta Party, a youth-led protest movement, held talks with India's government on July 24 after activist Sonam Wangchuk ended a 26-day hunger strike. The negotiation marks a pivot from weeks of street pressure to formal engagement, though the government has not conceded the protesters' central demand.

Wangchuk, a climate activist and founder of an education nonprofit, suspended his fast after receiving assurances on three fronts: no legal action against protesters at Jantar Mantar or those who marched to parliament; withdrawal of police cases against demonstrators; and parliamentary discussion of the National Eligibility cum Entrance Test (NEET) paper leak, examination system accountability, and broader exam reforms. He stated he received no specific promise on Pradhan's resignation, according to BBC reporting.

The government, through federal minister JP Nadda, said it would present its decision on the minister's fate on Saturday during a second round of talks, per CJP spokesperson Saurav Das. CJP spokesperson Ashutosh Ranka told BBC that the government asked for a day to decide on the resignation demand.

The protests began over a high-profile exam fraud case. On May 3, nearly 2.28 million candidates sat the NEET medical entrance exam. Days later, the government cancelled it after the question paper leaked, and held a re-test. The leak triggered the initial demonstrations and, per BBC reporting, students reportedly died by suicide after the exam's cancellation. The government has agreed to offer compensation to families of those students, according to Ranka.

Prime Minister Narendra Modi posted a video promising a tough new law on examination paper leaks, saying he had received a draft legislation he wants to introduce soon in parliament. "It causes immense distress to hundreds of thousands of students and their parents," Modi said in the video, according to BBC reporting.

Education Minister Pradhan has not commented directly on the resignation demand. He previously criticized the opposition Congress party for "shamelessly exploiting students as political tools to manufacture disruption," per BBC's report. CJP co-founder Abhijeet Dipke posted that Wangchuk's hunger strike had "awakened the conscience of an entire nation."

The CJP emerged in May from a comment by India's chief justice comparing unemployed young people drifting toward journalism and activism with cockroaches and parasites. The movement has since grown into one of India's most visible youth mobilizations and one of the largest public displays of dissent against the Modi government since it took office in 2014, according to BBC reporting. Hundreds of young protesters spent the night on roads and pavements at Jantar Mantar chanting slogans demanding Pradhan's resignation.

Authorities forcibly removed Wangchuk from the protest site the previous Saturday and admitted him to hospital citing health concerns. Two days later, Delhi police cracked down on protesters attempting to march to parliament, sparking what BBC describes as massive public anger. Security in the area has been tightened with heavy police presence, mobile internet suspended in several areas, and 18 metro stations closed.
